channel.title: Recent & Upcoming Talks | Leo Y. Yang

item.title: AI and Social Science Interdisciplinary Seminar
item.description:
Invited by Xi Weng at the Guanghua School of Management, Peking University, I presented at the “AI and Social Science” interdisciplinary seminar series.

Talk: Scaling Reproducibility

Computational reproducibility is central to scientific credibility, yet verifying published results at scale remains costly. I introduced an AI-assisted workflow for automated full-paper replication — retrieving materials, reconstructing environments, executing code, and matching outputs to point estimates reported in regression tables.

Key findings from the study:

  • Universe definition. We define a universe of all empirical and quantitative papers from the three top political science journals (2010–2025) and measure stated data availability using automated extraction.
  • Replication at scale. For a stratified sample of 384 studies, we apply the workflow to conduct full-paper replication, totaling 3,382 empirical models.
  • Impact of mandates. Journal verification requirements, combined with data archiving mandates, drive reproducibility: the full-paper reproducibility rate rises from 29.6% before DA-RT adoption to 79.8% after, and conditional on accessible replication packages, 94.4% of papers are fully reproducible (237/251).
  • IV diagnostics. As a secondary application, we apply standardized IV diagnostics to 92 studies (215 specifications), illustrating how automated execution enables systematic reanalysis across heterogeneous empirical settings.

item.title: AI + Social Science Workshop
item.description:
I joined the tenth Luojia EMS Frontier Methods Bootcamp & AI + Social Science Workshop to exchange ideas on how generative AI reshapes empirical research in the social sciences. Hosted by the School of Economics and Management at Wuhan University, the workshop convened Raymond Duch, Lijia Wei, Lian Xue, Peiran Jiao, and other colleagues for a full day of methodological training and research dialogue.

Session highlights

  • 08:30-10:00 - Frontier methods overview. Raymond Duch outlined how LLMs can act as experimental subjects, pollsters, and market analysts, followed by Lijia Wei’s evidence on the Matching-LLM framework for forecasting public opinion.
  • 10:20-11:50 - Research methods training. Lian Xue discussed belief updating with medical AI explanations, and Peiran Jiao linked employee voice data from Glassdoor to firm valuation models.
  • 11:50-12:35 - My presentation. I presented Predictive Power, Bias, and Trust: Evaluating LLM-Driven Screening in FinTech Lending, showing how model governance and human oversight mitigate screening bias.
  • 14:00-16:00 - Roundtable discussion. Speakers stayed on for a closed-door session to trade implementation lessons on building trustworthy AI workflows for social-science data.

These conversations fed directly into my ongoing AIECON collaborations on data-centric AI, regulation, and human-centered evaluation of large language models.

item.title: High-Frequency Social Media Data Seminar
item.description:
Invited by the Jinan University Social Survey Center, I delivered Lecture 55 in the “Social Survey & Application” series on the policy shock created by Weibo’s April 2022 IP disclosure requirement. The project tracks 200 news accounts and the top comment chains around each post to observe how geography-linked identity cues steer civic debate.

Research overview

  • Data engineering. I detailed the pipeline we built to capture pre/post policy posts, scrape reply trees, and annotate user locations while protecting user privacy.
  • Behavioral impact. Comparing domestic and overseas commenters reveals divergent participation patterns once location tags become visible, alongside a measurable drop in hostile replies.
  • Implications for moderators. Location transparency changes how online communities interpret credibility and allegiances, so we discussed how to pair machine flags with human review to keep deliberation open while curbing abuse.

We closed with a Q&A on fielding API-rate constraints, validating location cues, and extending the design to other high-frequency social platforms.
